      SPAC clawbacks!
   =20
 =20

A special purpose acquisition company is a clever bit of financial engineer=
ing. A sponsor creates a shell company =E2=80=94=C2=A0the SPAC =E2=80=94 th=
at does a public stock offering and sells stock for $10 per share. Let=E2=
=80=99s say she sells 10 million shares for a total of $100 million. The mo=
ney goes into a pot, and the sponsor goes and looks for a private company t=
o take public. If she finds one, the SPAC merges with the target company, t=
he target company gets the $100 million and the SPAC=E2=80=99s public stock=
-exchange listing, and the shareholders of the SPAC get shares of the newly=
 public target company. Or, if they don=E2=80=99t want those shares, they c=
an take back their $10, with interest, when the merger closes.=C2=A0The spo=
nsor generally gets 20% of the SPAC=E2=80=99s shares, for free,=C2=A0as a r=
eward for her efforts; here she would get shares worth about $25 million.
  [1]

But if the sponsor doesn=E2=80=99t find a target and do a deal by a fixed d=
eadline =E2=80=94=C2=A0generally two years after the SPAC goes public =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0the SPAC liquidates and hands the money back to its shareholder=
s. In this scenario, the sponsor gets nothing. In fact, she generally=C2=A0=
loses=C2=A0money. The SPAC has some costs: It has to pay investment bankers=
 for its initial public offering, and then pay for accountants and consulta=
nts and a website and whatever as it searches for a target.=C2=A0The $100 m=
illion that the SPAC raised from public shareholders can=E2=80=99t be touch=
ed to pay these costs: It has to keep that money in trust to use for an acq=
uisition, or to pay back to its investors if it can=E2=80=99t close a deal.=
 The investors have to get their $10 back, generally with interest, though=
=C2=A0some=C2=A0of the interest can generally be used to cover a few specif=
ic costs.
  [2]

This means that the sponsor is effectively on the hook for most of the star=
tup and operating costs of the SPAC, and she provides some startup capital =
to the SPAC to fund these costs. If she finds a deal and it closes, she wil=
l get stock that is generally worth many times her investment. But if she d=
oesn=E2=80=99t, she=E2=80=99ll be out probably a couple million bucks.=C2=
=A0During the 2020-2021 SPAC boom, a lot of people got into the business of=
 sponsoring SPACs because it looked like free money, and then the boom coll=
apsed and   they lost money.
This structure makes SPAC shares sort of a weird investment.=C2=A0From the =
perspective of a public shareholder, the shares can look like a=C2=A0money =
market fund. (=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s Official: SPACs Are the New Money-Marke=
t Funds,=E2=80=9D  said the Wall Street Journal a year ago.) You put in $10=
, the company parks that money in Treasury bills, and you are guaranteed to=
 get back your $10 with interest within two years. And SPACs have been tout=
ed as a fixed-income investment: There was a time when a lot of more-or-les=
s hopeless SPACs traded at, like, $9.90 per share, and you could buy them a=
nd get your money back with slightly-above-market interest within a few mon=
ths, and that was a popular trade.
There is a sort of financial and legal sleight of hand going on here. You a=
re buying=C2=A0common stock=C2=A0in a company with no operating business, r=
un by some sponsors whom you don=E2=80=99t know and who are trying to get r=
ich quick. That=C2=A0sounds=C2=A0like a very risky investment. But regulato=
rs and gatekeepers are very attuned to that risk, and so SPACs do seem to b=
e pretty carefully policed to make sure that they keep the money safe. The =
money is generally deposited with a reputable trustee, invested in bank acc=
ounts and Treasuries, and not accessible to the SPAC=E2=80=99s sponsors or =
executives. Today the US Securities and Exchange Commission  brought an enf=
orcement action against a SPAC, African Gold Acquisition Corp., that carele=
ssly =E2=80=9Cenabled African Gold=E2=80=99s former chief financial officer=
 to misappropriate approximately $1.2 million from the company=E2=80=99s op=
erating bank account.=E2=80=9D  But =E2=80=9CAfrican Gold=E2=80=99s former =
CFO did not have access to African Gold=E2=80=99s trust account and did not=
 misappropriate any funds from the trust account=E2=80=9D: Even the SPACs w=
hose executives are actively stealing money from the company manage to keep=
 their shareholders=E2=80=99 $10 safe.=C2=A0
Anyway the SPAC boom ended and a lot of SPACs ended up returning money to s=
hareholders. But now some of them are =E2=80=A6=C2=A0asking for some of it =
back? Oops?=C2=A0
So here is Thunder Bridge Capital Partners III=C2=A0Inc., a SPAC that  rais=
ed $414 million in February 2021.
  [3]
 Its deadline for doing a deal would have expired earlier this month, but T=
hunder Bridge III asked its shareholders to approve an extension until Augu=
st, and the shareholders approved. The way this generally works is that the=
 shareholders have the right to redeem their stock at the time of the exten=
sion, and they basically all did: An overwhelming majority of the SPAC=E2=
=80=99s shareholders  voted for the extension, but then almost all of them =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0holders of 40.3 million out of 41.4 million public shares =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cexercised their right to redeem their shares for ca=
sh at a redemption price of approximately $10.10 per share, for an aggregat=
e redemption amount of approximately $407 million.=E2=80=9D So the SPAC wen=
t from having  about $416 million in its trust account to having about $9 m=
illion. I don=E2=80=99t know what kind of merger it is going to do with $9 =
million but never mind.
Here, however, is an announcement that Thunder Bridge III=C2=A0 filed last =
Friday:

On December 30, 2022, an initial redemption payment was made by Continental=
 Stock Transfer & Trust Company (=E2=80=9CCST=E2=80=9D), as trustee of the =
trust account set up at the time of the Company=E2=80=99s initial public of=
fering for the benefit of the Company=E2=80=99s public stockholders (the =
=E2=80=9CTrust Account=E2=80=9D), to the Redeeming Stockholders at a rate o=
f $10.10 per share and, on January 11, 2023, CST made an additional redempt=
ion payment (the =E2=80=9CAdditional Payment=E2=80=9D) to the Redeeming Sto=
ckholders at a rate of $0.02841302 per share, for a total redemption paymen=
t per share of $10.12841302. It was later determined that the Company did n=
ot withdraw all of the interest from the Trust Account that it was allowed =
to withdraw to cover income and franchise taxes and, therefore, the Additio=
nal Payment should have been $0.00157381 per share, for a total redemption =
payment of $10.10157381 per share. This meant that the Redeeming Stockholde=
rs were overpaid in the amount of $0.02683921 per share (the =E2=80=9COverp=
ayment Amount=E2=80=9D).
The Redeeming Stockholders are in the process of being notified of this sit=
uation and are being instructed to return the Overpayment Amount to CST.

If you owned stock in Thunder Bridge III and cashed out, you got paid $10.1=
28 per share, but you apparently=C2=A0should=C2=A0have gotten $10.101 per s=
hare, so you owe them back about $0.027 per share. This comes to about $1,0=
81,672 total, which is like 10% of the money left in the pot, so I guess it=
=E2=80=99s worth going after.=C2=A0
Or there is Industrial Human Capital Inc., a SPAC that  raised $115 million=
 in October 2021. It had a tight 12-month deadline to do a deal, which it m=
issed.=C2=A0It  asked shareholders for an extension and, as in the case of =
Thunder Bridge III, the shareholders approved the extension but almost all =
of them asked for their money back. Industrial Human Capital was like =E2=
=80=9Cehh never mind then, let=E2=80=99s just wind up,=E2=80=9D  so they di=
d, and paid out about $10.22=C2=A0per share =E2=80=94=C2=A0all the money in=
 the trust account =E2=80=94 in December.
  [4]

The problem is that Industrial Human Capital still owed some money to some =
vendors.=C2=A0On Feb. 7, a few creditors =E2=80=94=C2=A0Berkowitz Pollack B=
rant Advisors (accountants, though not Industrial Human Capital=E2=80=99s a=
uditors), Effectus Group LLC (also apparently accountants) and Cargas Syste=
ms Inc. (accounting software providers) =E2=80=94=C2=A0filed an involuntary=
 bankruptcy petition against Industrial Human Capital, seeking to get paid =
for about $1 million of unpaid invoices.=C2=A0
And so Industrial Human Capital also seems to be asking shareholders for so=
me money back, about $0.11 per share to cover the missing million dollars; =
here is, apparently,  a copy of the letter.
  [5]
 Industrial Human Capital exhausted all of its money that was=C2=A0not=C2=
=A0in the trust (including some money that its sponsor had loaned to the SP=
AC; the sponsor has waived its right to be repaid for those loans), and sti=
ll had some bills left over. It actually tried to take that money out of th=
e trust, but the trustee said no. From the letter:
The Company and some of the creditors holding significant claims made deman=
ds on Continental Stock Transfer & Trust Company (as the =E2=80=9CTrustee=
=E2=80=9D of the =E2=80=9CTrust Account=E2=80=9D) for payment of the Capita=
l Deficiency before the redemptions were effected, but the Trustee refused =
to make the payment, because the Investment Management Trust Agreement betw=
een the Company and the Trustee, dated as of October 19, 2021 (the =E2=80=
=9CTrust Agreement=E2=80=9D), did not allow for such payment (it allowed on=
ly for the payment to the Company of certain taxes and up to $100,000 in di=
ssolution expenses). The Trustee did not want to be subject to claims of br=
each of the Trust Agreement.
See, that=E2=80=99s good: The SPAC went to its trustee and said =E2=80=9Chi=
 we have legitimate vendor expenses and we=E2=80=99d like to use some of th=
e interest on the trust account to pay them back,=E2=80=9D and the trustee =
said =E2=80=9Cabsolutely not, you are not taking a penny from your sharehol=
ders beyond what we have explicitly agreed to.=E2=80=9D The SPAC system wor=
ked, the trustee protected the money in the pot, and the shareholders got a=
ll their money back.
Except that the SPAC system is sort of a sleight of hand, and those shareho=
lders=C2=A0are=C2=A0still shareholders. They owned=C2=A0stock=C2=A0of a com=
pany, and the company=E2=80=99s creditors are supposed to get their money b=
ack before the shareholders do.=C2=A0 So the SPAC and the creditors agreed =
that the trust would pay out $10.22 per share to shareholders, and then the=
y=E2=80=99d ask the shareholders for some of the money =E2=80=94=C2=A0$0.11=
 per share =E2=80=94=C2=A0back.
As the letter says, that=E2=80=99s legal! Delaware law says that the liabil=
ity of a shareholder of a dissolved corporation =E2=80=9Cshall not exceed t=
he amount distributed to such stockholder in dissolution,=E2=80=9D implying=
 that creditors of the corporation=C2=A0can=C2=A0go after the shareholders =
for anything up to the $10.22 per share that they got.
  [6]
 And the  risk factors in the SPAC=E2=80=99s original prospectus =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0which are boilerplate found in every SPAC prospectus=C2=A0=E2=80=94=
=C2=A0explicitly warn:

If, after we distribute the proceeds in the trust account to our public sto=
ckholders, we file a bankruptcy petition or an involuntary bankruptcy petit=
ion is filed against us that is not dismissed, a bankruptcy court may seek =
to recover such proceeds ....
If, after we distribute the proceeds in the trust account to our public sto=
ckholders, we file a bankruptcy petition or an involuntary bankruptcy petit=
ion is filed against us that is not dismissed, any distributions received b=
y stockholders could be viewed under applicable debtor/creditor and/or bank=
ruptcy laws as either a =E2=80=9Cpreferential transfer=E2=80=9D or a =E2=80=
=9Cfraudulent conveyance.=E2=80=9D As a result, a bankruptcy court could se=
ek to recover some or all amounts received by our stockholders. ...
Our stockholders may be held liable for claims by third parties against us =
to the extent of distributions received by them upon redemption of their sh=
ares.

The shareholders were warned! They are shareholders, they are junior to the=
 creditors, and if the company owes money to creditors then they do not get=
 all their money back.
A couple of points here. First of all, clearly the=C2=A0polite=C2=A0thing f=
or the SPAC to do here is for the sponsor to eat the extra million-dollar c=
ost. After all, the sponsor ran=C2=A0the SPAC; it decided to have the SPAC =
hire the vendors that it couldn=E2=80=99t pay. The public shareholders of t=
he SPAC didn=E2=80=99t decide to hire these accountants, the sponsor did, s=
o the sponsor should be on the hook. The essential=C2=A0deal=C2=A0of the SP=
AC is that the sponsor raises money from public shareholders, puts the mone=
y in a pot to keep it safe for the shareholders, and only spends it to clos=
e a deal with a target company; any expenses that the SPAC incurs along the=
 way are the sponsor=E2=80=99s problem.
And in fact the prospectus mostly says that. From the risk factors:

Our placing of funds in the trust account may not protect those funds from =
third-party claims against us. Although we will seek to have all vendors, s=
ervice providers, prospective target businesses and other entities with whi=
ch we do business execute agreements with us waiving any right, title, inte=
rest or claim of any kind in or to any monies held in the trust account for=
 the benefit of our public stockholders, such parties may not execute such =
agreements. =E2=80=A6 If any third party refuses to execute an agreement wa=
iving such claims to the monies held in the trust account, our management w=
ill perform an analysis of the alternatives available to it and will only e=
nter into an agreement with a third party that has not executed a waiver if=
 management believes that such third party=E2=80=99s engagement would be si=
gnificantly more beneficial to us than any alternative. ...
Examples of possible instances where we may engage a third party that refus=
es to execute a waiver include the engagement of a third party consultant w=
hose particular expertise or skills are believed by management to be signif=
icantly superior to those of other consultants that would agree to execute =
a waiver or in cases where management is unable to find a service provider =
willing to execute a waiver. ...=C2=A0Upon redemption of our public shares =
=E2=80=A6 we will be required to provide for payment of claims of creditors=
 that were not waived that may be brought against us within the 10 years fo=
llowing redemption. Accordingly, the per-share redemption amount received b=
y public stockholders could be less than the $10.15 per share initially hel=
d in the trust account, due to claims of such creditors. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0Our=
 sponsor has agreed that it will be liable to us if and to the extent any c=
laims by a third party for services rendered or products sold to us =E2=80=
=A6 reduce the amount of funds in the trust account to below the lesser of =
(i) $10.15 per public share and (ii) the actual amount per public share hel=
d in the trust account as of the date of the liquidation of the trust accou=
nt, if less than $10.15 per share.=C2=A0

That is:=C2=A0
=09The SPAC will try to get its vendors to agree not to go after the money =
in the trust.=09Failing that, the sponsor agreed to pay any vendors=E2=80=
=99 bills, if those bills would reduce the money in the trust below $10.15 =
per share.

But the trust actually returned $10.22 per share, so the sponsor is not on =
the hook for the extra $0.07. (Why the SPAC thinks that shareholders have t=
o pay back $0.11, rather than getting $0.07 from the shareholders and the r=
est from the sponsor, I don=E2=80=99t know!) And, the risk factor goes on, =
=E2=80=9Cwe have not asked our sponsor to reserve for such indemnification =
obligations, nor have we independently verified whether our sponsor has suf=
ficient funds to satisfy its indemnity obligations and believe that our spo=
nsor=E2=80=99s only assets are securities of our company.=E2=80=9D That is,=
 this is just an unsecured obligation of the sponsor =E2=80=94=C2=A0which a=
s it happens is a subsidiary of ShiftPixy Inc., a $50 million market cap pu=
blic company =E2=80=94=C2=A0and there=E2=80=99s no guarantee that they=E2=
=80=99re good for it.
The other point I want to make is that=C2=A0this is not how money market fu=
nds work. That is: In=C2=A0most=C2=A0situations =E2=80=94=C2=A0even situati=
ons like African Gold, where the executives are stealing from the SPAC =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0the shareholders=E2=80=99 money in the SPAC really is safe, and=
 they are guaranteed to get their $10 back with interest. But=C2=A0this=C2=
=A0points to a situation where that isn=E2=80=99t true: If the SPAC owes mo=
ney to legitimate vendors, those vendors are creditors who outrank the shar=
eholders, and who can claim money back from the shareholders. The trustee o=
f the pot of money won=E2=80=99t give it to them =E2=80=94=C2=A0the trustee=
 is laser-focused on keeping the shareholders=E2=80=99 money safe =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0but a court probably will. Courts know that SPAC shareholders are sha=
reholders, and that creditors outrank them.
Here it=E2=80=99s 11 cents: The shareholders put in $10, got back $10.22, m=
ight have to pay back $0.11, and so end up with $10.11. They still get thei=
r money back with interest. But that=E2=80=99s because the vendor bills are=
 small. You could imagine another SPAC with a worse outcome: a big vendor b=
ill that eats up all of the interest on the trust account and dips into pri=
ncipal, and a sponsor who is not good for the money. That is not very likel=
y =E2=80=94=C2=A0SPACs tend not to run up huge vendor bills, and if you are=
 a vendor selling millions of dollars of stuff to a SPAC you will probably =
want to do some due diligence to make sure it has the money to pay you =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0but it is possible. Empirically, in the SPAC boom of the 2020s,=
 SPACs have been very good at giving shareholders back their $10. But that =
is not really a guarantee.

en, you know, great. If it is not true =E2=80=94=C2=A0if your assets are tr=
oubled and the run is ongoing =E2=80=94=C2=A0then, well, that=E2=80=99s why=
 they pay you the big bucks.=C2=A0If you go on television and=C2=A0say=C2=

 You are the captain of the ship, which most days is a nice job and pays we=

 =E2=80=94=C2=A0but you=E2=80=99d never=C2=A0say=C2=A0it. You can=E2=80=99t=
 go around telling bank executives that it=E2=80=99s okay to lie about thei=

Elsewhere in modern financial regulatory expectations:

Europe=E2=80=99s top banking regulators have told Deutsche Bank they are =
=E2=80=9Cnot satisfied=E2=80=9D with its probe into the mis-selling of risk=
y foreign exchange derivatives in Spain, people briefed on the matter told =
the Financial Times.
Both the European Central Bank and German watchdog BaFin have expressed fru=
stration with the probe =E2=80=94 code-named =E2=80=9CProject Teal=E2=80=9D=
 =E2=80=94 which began in the second half of 2019 and is about to be conclu=
ded, people familiar with the matter said.
The investigation found that Deutsche staff acted disingenuously, exploited=
 flaws in the bank=E2=80=99s controls and broke EU rules to sell highly com=
plex foreign exchange derivatives to small and medium-sized Spanish compani=
es, the FT reported this month.
However, regulators chided the group over several issues, including shortco=
mings in methodology, as well as the length of time it took to conduct the =
probe and then to punish those concerned. One person familiar with the matt=
er said one of the points criticised by regulators was that the bank only s=
crutinised a limited number of transactions when it checked the quality of =
its internal controls, arguing that taking a broader sample would have been=
 a better approach.

The traditional view of bank regulation is something like:
=09Banks do whatever they can=C2=A0to make a lot of money.=09Regulators che=
ck to make sure that they=E2=80=99re not making money in bad ways, and if t=
hey are, they fine them.

But the modern view of global bank regulation is more like:
=09Banks=E2=80=99 business units do whatever they can to make a lot of mone=
y.=09Banks=E2=80=99 compliance, risk, etc., units check to make sure that t=
he business units aren=E2=80=99t making money in bad ways, and if they are,=
 they turn them in to the regulators with a big show of contrition (and a b=
ig fine).=09Regulators check to make sure that the compliance units are app=
ropriately watchful and/or contrite, and if they aren=E2=80=99t, they fine =
them.

The banks regulate themselves, and the regulators do   sort of meta-regulat=
ion of the banks=E2=80=99 regulatory work. It is more efficient to delegate=
 the regulation to the banks: The banks have bigger budgets, so they can hi=
re more compliance staff, and the compliance staff sit in the same building=
 and probably have better access to the business units than the regulators =
do. On the other hand they probably have=C2=A0slightly=C2=A0less incentive =
to be aggressive than the regulators do.=C2=A0

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">SPAC clawbacks!</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A special purpose acquisition company is a cle=
ver bit of financial engineering. A sponsor creates a shell company =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0the SPAC =E2=80=94 that does a public stock offering and sells sto=
ck for $10 per share. Let=E2=80=99s say she sells 10 million shares for a t=
otal of $100 million. The money goes into a pot, and the sponsor goes and l=
ooks for a private company to take public. If she finds one, the SPAC merge=
s with the target company, the target company gets the $100 million and the=
 SPAC=E2=80=99s public stock-exchange listing, and the shareholders of the =
SPAC get shares of the newly public target company. Or, if they don=E2=80=
=99t want those shares, they can take back their $10, with interest, when t=
he merger closes.=C2=A0The sponsor generally gets 20% of the SPAC=E2=80=99s=
 shares, for free,=C2=A0as a reward for her efforts; here she would get sha=
res worth about $25 million.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1=
" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-=
width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !import=
ant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But if the sponsor doesn=E2=80=99t find a targ=
et and do a deal by a fixed deadline =E2=80=94=C2=A0generally two years aft=
er the SPAC goes public =E2=80=94=C2=A0the SPAC liquidates and hands the mo=
ney back to its shareholders. In this scenario, the sponsor gets nothing. I=
n fact, she generally=C2=A0<em>loses</em>=C2=A0money. The SPAC has some cos=
ts: It has to pay investment bankers for its initial public offering, and t=
hen pay for accountants and consultants and a website and whatever as it se=
arches for a target.=C2=A0The $100 million that the SPAC raised from public=
 shareholders can=E2=80=99t be touched to pay these costs: It has to keep t=
hat money in trust to use for an acquisition, or to pay back to its investo=
rs if it can=E2=80=99t close a deal. The investors have to get their $10 ba=
ck, generally with interest, though=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</em>of the interest=
 can generally be used to cover a few specific costs.<a class=3D"footnote-i=
ndex" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !=
important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-b=
ottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; dis=
play: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This means that the sponsor is effectively on =
the hook for most of the startup and operating costs of the SPAC, and she p=
rovides some startup capital to the SPAC to fund these costs. If she finds =
a deal and it closes, she will get stock that is generally worth many times=
 her investment. But if she doesn=E2=80=99t, she=E2=80=99ll be out probably=
 a couple million bucks.=C2=A0During the 2020-2021 SPAC boom, a lot of peop=
le got into the business of sponsoring SPACs because it looked like free mo=
ney, and then the boom collapsed and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDUtMjAvdGhlLXNwYWMtYnVzdC1pcy1leHBlbnNpdmU_Y21waWQ=
9QkJEMDIyMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZX=
R0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwMjIyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cBb6dc446a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">  they lost money</a>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This structure makes SPAC shares sort of a wei=
rd investment.=C2=A0From the perspective of a public shareholder, the share=
s can look like a=C2=A0<em>money market fund</em>. (=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s O=
fficial: SPACs Are the New Money-Market Funds,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqL=
mNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9pdHMtb2ZmaWNpYWwtc3BhY3MtYXJlLXRoZS1uZXctbW9uZXktbWFya2V0=
LWZ1bmRzLTExNjQ0NDA0MzQ4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBee64d8cb" itemprop=3D"Sto=
ryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; =
text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-c=
olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> said t=
he Wall Street Journal</a> a year ago.) You put in $10, the company parks t=
hat money in Treasury bills, and you are guaranteed to get back your $10 wi=
th interest within two years. And SPACs have been touted as a fixed-income =
investment: There was a time when a lot of more-or-less hopeless SPACs trad=
ed at, like, $9.90 per share, and you could buy them and get your money bac=
k with slightly-above-market interest within a few months, and that was a p=
opular trade.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is a sort of financial and legal sleight=
 of hand going on here. You are buying=C2=A0<em>common stock=C2=A0</em>in a=
 company with no operating business, run by some sponsors whom you don=E2=
=80=99t know and who are trying to get rich quick. That=C2=A0<em>sounds=C2=
=A0</em>like a very risky investment. But regulators and gatekeepers are ve=
ry attuned to that risk, and so SPACs do seem to be pretty carefully police=
d to make sure that they keep the money safe. The money is generally deposi=
ted with a reputable trustee, invested in bank accounts and Treasuries, and=
 not accessible to the SPAC=E2=80=99s sponsors or executives. Today the US =
Securities and Exchange Commission <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbu=
siness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLX=
JlbGVhc2UvMjAyMy0zNg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5cb99db6" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> brought an=
 enforcement action</a> against a SPAC, African Gold Acquisition Corp., tha=
t carelessly =E2=80=9Cenabled African Gold=E2=80=99s former chief financial=
 officer to misappropriate approximately $1.2 million from the company=E2=
=80=99s operating bank account.=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0=
aW9uL2FkbWluLzIwMjMvMzQtOTY5NjAucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB94d55b94" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;"> But</a> =E2=80=9CAfrican Gold=E2=80=99s former CFO did not have acces=
s to African Gold=E2=80=99s trust account and did not misappropriate any fu=
nds from the trust account=E2=80=9D: Even the SPACs whose executives are ac=
tively stealing money from the company manage to keep their shareholders=E2=
=80=99 $10 safe.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway the SPAC boom ended and a lot of SPACs =
ended up returning money to shareholders. But now some of them are =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0asking for some of it back? Oops?=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So here is Thunder Bridge Capital Partners III=
=C2=A0Inc., a SPAC that <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhL=
zE4MTU3NTMvMDAwMTIxMzkwMDIxMDA3NjA0L2Y0MjRiNDAyMjFfdGh1bmRlcmJyaWRnZTMuaHRt=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB365fde57" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> raised $414 million</a> in Feb=
ruary 2021.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-c=
olor: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> Its deadline for doing a deal would have expired earlier this month, b=
ut Thunder Bridge III asked its shareholders to approve an extension until =
August, and the shareholders approved. The way this generally works is that=
 the shareholders have the right to redeem their stock at the time of the e=
xtension, and they basically all did: An overwhelming majority of the SPAC=
=E2=80=99s shareholders <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ=
2FyL2RhdGEvMTgxNTc1My8wMDAxMjEzOTAwMjIwODIyMDIvZWExNzAzMTYtOGtfdGh1bmRlcmJy=
aTMuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB03e9bfdc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscop=
e=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration:=
 none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; b=
order-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> voted for the extensio=
n</a>, but then almost all of them =E2=80=94=C2=A0holders of 40.3 million o=
ut of 41.4 million public shares =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cexercised their ri=
ght to redeem their shares for cash at a redemption price of approximately =
$10.10 per share, for an aggregate redemption amount of approximately $407 =
million.=E2=80=9D So the SPAC went from having <a href=3D"https://link.mail=
.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD=
9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTgxNTc1My8wMDAxMjEzOTAwMjIwNzIxNDUvZjEwc=
TA5MjJfdGh1bmRlcmJyaWRnZTMuaHRtI2FfMDAz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB67565ace" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;"> about $416 million</a> in its trust account to having about $9 mil=
lion. I don=E2=80=99t know what kind of merger it is going to do with $9 mi=
llion but never mind.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here, however, is an announcement that Thunder=
 Bridge III=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3=
0629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2Rh=
dGEvMTgxNTc1My8wMDAxMjEzOTAwMjMwMTI4MzUvZWExNzM5MzUtOGtfdGh1bmRlcmlpaS5odG0=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC0eae3d25" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> filed last Friday</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On December 30, 2022, an initial redemption pa=
yment was made by Continental Stock Transfer & Trust Company (=E2=80=9C=
CST=E2=80=9D), as trustee of the trust account set up at the time of the Co=
mpany=E2=80=99s initial public offering for the benefit of the Company=E2=
=80=99s public stockholders (the =E2=80=9CTrust Account=E2=80=9D), to the R=
edeeming Stockholders at a rate of $10.10 per share and, on January 11, 202=
3, CST made an additional redemption payment (the =E2=80=9CAdditional Payme=
nt=E2=80=9D) to the Redeeming Stockholders at a rate of $0.02841302 per sha=
re, for a total redemption payment per share of $10.12841302. It was later =
determined that the Company did not withdraw all of the interest from the T=
rust Account that it was allowed to withdraw to cover income and franchise =
taxes and, therefore, the Additional Payment should have been $0.00157381 p=
er share, for a total redemption payment of $10.10157381 per share. This me=
ant that the Redeeming Stockholders were overpaid in the amount of $0.02683=
921 per share (the =E2=80=9COverpayment Amount=E2=80=9D).</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Redeeming Stockholders are in the process =
of being notified of this situation and are being instructed to return the =
Overpayment Amount to CST.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you owned stock in Thunder Bridge III and c=
ashed out, you got paid $10.128 per share, but you apparently=C2=A0<em>shou=
ld=C2=A0</em>have gotten $10.101 per share, so you owe them back about $0.0=
27 per share. This comes to about $1,081,672 total, which is like 10% of th=
e money left in the pot, so I guess it=E2=80=99s worth going after.=C2=A0</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or there is Industrial Human Capital Inc., a S=
PAC that <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.=
283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4NTUzMDIvMDAw=
MTEwNDY1OTIxMTI4NDIxL3RtMjEzMDc0MGQxXzQyNGI0Lmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
B897d008c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;"> raised $115 million</a> in October 2021. It had a tight=
 12-month deadline to do a deal, which it missed.=C2=A0It <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2V=
jLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4NTUzMDIvMDAwMTEwNDY1OTIyMTAxMjI4L3RtMj=
IyNjExOWQxX2RlZjE0YS5odG0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4930221e" itemprop=3D"St=
oryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> asked=
 shareholders for an extension</a> and, as in the case of Thunder Bridge II=
I, the shareholders approved the extension but almost all of them asked for=
 their money back. Industrial Human Capital was like =E2=80=9Cehh never min=
d then, let=E2=80=99s just wind up,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.b=
loombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9k=
b2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTg1NTMwMi8wMDAxMTA0NjU5MjIxMTgzNzQvdG0yMjM=
wNDUxZDFfOGsuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cD7388245a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink"=
 itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> so they did</=
a>, and paid out about $10.22=C2=A0per share =E2=80=94=C2=A0all the money i=
n the trust account =E2=80=94 in December.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The problem is that Industrial Human Capital s=
till owed some money to some vendors.=C2=A0On Feb. 7, a few creditors =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0Berkowitz Pollack Brant Advisors (accountants, though not Indus=
trial Human Capital=E2=80=99s auditors), Effectus Group LLC (also apparentl=
y accountants) and Cargas Systems Inc. (accounting software providers) =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0filed <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0h=
CRmRmeElVL3J1VDV6bUUxMnVZZy92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBa430b376" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
an involuntary bankruptcy petition</a> against Industrial Human Capital, se=
eking to get paid for about $1 million of unpaid invoices.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so Industrial Human Capital also seems to =
be asking shareholders for some money back, about $0.11 per share to cover =
the missing million dollars; here is, apparently, <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9=
NaWR3ZXN0SGVkZ2llL3N0YXR1cy8xNjI4Mzk3Mzk4MTExMTg2OTQ0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a=
23cB7a3414e5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bla=
nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;"> a copy of the letter</a>.<a class=3D"footnote-index"=
 href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display:=
 inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a> Industrial Human Capital exhausted all of its money that was=C2=A0<em>=
not=C2=A0</em>in the trust (including some money that its sponsor had loane=
d to the SPAC; the sponsor has waived its right to be repaid for those loan=
s), and still had some bills left over. It actually tried to take that mone=
y out of the trust, but the trustee said no. From the letter:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Company and some of the credit=
ors holding significant claims made demands on Continental Stock Transfer &=
amp; Trust Company (as the =E2=80=9CTrustee=E2=80=9D of the =E2=80=9CTrust =
Account=E2=80=9D) for payment of the Capital Deficiency before the redempti=
ons were effected, but the Trustee refused to make the payment, because the=
 Investment Management Trust Agreement between the Company and the Trustee,=
 dated as of October 19, 2021 (the =E2=80=9CTrust Agreement=E2=80=9D), did =
not allow for such payment (it allowed only for the payment to the Company =
of certain taxes and up to $100,000 in dissolution expenses). The Trustee d=
id not want to be subject to claims of breach of the Trust Agreement.</p></=
blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">See, that=E2=80=99s good: The SPAC went to its=
 trustee and said =E2=80=9Chi we have legitimate vendor expenses and we=E2=
=80=99d like to use some of the interest on the trust account to pay them b=
ack,=E2=80=9D and the trustee said =E2=80=9Cabsolutely not, you are not tak=
ing a penny from your shareholders beyond what we have explicitly agreed to=
.=E2=80=9D The SPAC system worked, the trustee protected the money in the p=
ot, and the shareholders got all their money back.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Except that the SPAC system is sort of a sleig=
ht of hand, and those shareholders=C2=A0<em>are=C2=A0</em>still shareholder=
s. They owned=C2=A0<em>stock=C2=A0</em>of a company, and the company=E2=80=
=99s creditors are supposed to get their money back before the shareholders=
 do.=C2=A0 So the SPAC and the creditors agreed that the trust would pay ou=
t $10.22 per share to shareholders, and then they=E2=80=99d ask the shareho=
lders for some of the money =E2=80=94=C2=A0$0.11 per share =E2=80=94=C2=A0b=
ack.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As the letter says, that=E2=80=99s legal! Dela=
ware law says that the liability of a shareholder of a dissolved corporatio=
n =E2=80=9Cshall not exceed the amount distributed to such stockholder in d=
issolution,=E2=80=9D implying that creditors of the corporation=C2=A0<em>ca=
n=C2=A0</em>go after the shareholders for anything up to the $10.22 per sha=
re that they got.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgr=
ound-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a> And the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629=
310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4NTUzMDIv=
MDAwMTEwNDY1OTIxMTI4NDIxL3RtMjEzMDc0MGQxXzQyNGI0Lmh0bSNhYV8wMDE/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB2c835768" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;"> risk factors</a> in the SPAC=E2=80=99s ori=
ginal prospectus =E2=80=94=C2=A0which are boilerplate found in every SPAC p=
rospectus=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0explicitly warn:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>If, after we distribute the proceeds i=
n the trust account to our public stockholders, we file a bankruptcy petiti=
on or an involuntary bankruptcy petition is filed against us that is not di=
smissed, a bankruptcy court may seek to recover such proceeds ....</strong>=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If, after we distribute the proceeds in the tr=
ust account to our public stockholders, we file a bankruptcy petition or an=
 involuntary bankruptcy petition is filed against us that is not dismissed,=
 any distributions received by stockholders could be viewed under applicabl=
e debtor/creditor and/or bankruptcy laws as either a =E2=80=9Cpreferential =
transfer=E2=80=9D or a =E2=80=9Cfraudulent conveyance.=E2=80=9D As a result=
, a bankruptcy court could seek to recover some or all amounts received by =
our stockholders. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><em><strong>Our stockholders may be held liabl=
e for claims by third parties against us to the extent of distributions rec=
eived by them upon redemption of their shares.</strong></em></p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The shareholders were warned! They are shareho=
lders, they are junior to the creditors, and if the company owes money to c=
reditors then they do not get all their money back.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A couple of points here. First of all, clearly=
 the=C2=A0<em>polite=C2=A0</em>thing for the SPAC to do here is for the spo=
nsor to eat the extra million-dollar cost. After all, the sponsor <em>ran=
=C2=A0</em>the SPAC; it decided to have the SPAC hire the vendors that it c=
ouldn=E2=80=99t pay. The public shareholders of the SPAC didn=E2=80=99t dec=
ide to hire these accountants, the sponsor did, so the sponsor should be on=
 the hook. The essential=C2=A0<em>deal=C2=A0</em>of the SPAC is that the sp=
onsor raises money from public shareholders, puts the money in a pot to kee=
p it safe for the shareholders, and only spends it to close a deal with a t=
arget company; any expenses that the SPAC incurs along the way are the spon=
sor=E2=80=99s problem.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And in fact the prospectus mostly says that. F=
rom the risk factors:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Our placing of funds in the trust account may =
not protect those funds from third-party claims against us. Although we wil=
l seek to have all vendors, service providers, prospective target businesse=
s and other entities with which we do business execute agreements with us w=
aiving any right, title, interest or claim of any kind in or to any monies =
held in the trust account for the benefit of our public stockholders, such =
parties may not execute such agreements. =E2=80=A6 If any third party refus=
es to execute an agreement waiving such claims to the monies held in the tr=
ust account, our management will perform an analysis of the alternatives av=
ailable to it and will only enter into an agreement with a third party that=
 has not executed a waiver if management believes that such third party=E2=
=80=99s engagement would be significantly more beneficial to us than any al=
ternative. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Examples of possible instances where we may en=
gage a third party that refuses to execute a waiver include the engagement =
of a third party consultant whose particular expertise or skills are believ=
ed by management to be significantly superior to those of other consultants=
 that would agree to execute a waiver or in cases where management is unabl=
e to find a service provider willing to execute a waiver. ...=C2=A0Upon red=
emption of our public shares =E2=80=A6 we will be required to provide for p=
ayment of claims of creditors that were not waived that may be brought agai=
nst us within the 10 years following redemption. Accordingly, the per-share=
 redemption amount received by public stockholders could be less than the $=
10.15 per share initially held in the trust account, due to claims of such =
creditors. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0Our sponsor has agreed that it will be liable to =
us if and to the extent any claims by a third party for services rendered o=
r products sold to us =E2=80=A6 reduce the amount of funds in the trust acc=
ount to below the lesser of (i) $10.15 per public share and (ii) the actual=
 amount per public share held in the trust account as of the date of the li=
quidation of the trust account, if less than $10.15 per share.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is:=C2=A0</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The SPAC will try to get its vendors =
to agree not to go after the money in the trust.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">Failing that, the sponsor agreed to pay any vendors=E2=80=99=
 bills, if those bills would reduce the money in the trust below $10.15 per=
 share.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the trust actually returned $10.22 per sha=
re, so the sponsor is not on the hook for the extra $0.07. (Why the SPAC th=
inks that shareholders have to pay back $0.11, rather than getting $0.07 fr=
om the shareholders and the rest from the sponsor, I don=E2=80=99t know!) A=
nd, the risk factor goes on, =E2=80=9Cwe have not asked our sponsor to rese=
rve for such indemnification obligations, nor have we independently verifie=
d whether our sponsor has sufficient funds to satisfy its indemnity obligat=
ions and believe that our sponsor=E2=80=99s only assets are securities of o=
ur company.=E2=80=9D That is, this is just an unsecured obligation of the s=
ponsor =E2=80=94=C2=A0which as it happens is a subsidiary of ShiftPixy Inc.=
, a $50 million market cap public company =E2=80=94=C2=A0and there=E2=80=99=
s no guarantee that they=E2=80=99re good for it.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other point I want to make is that=C2=A0<e=
m>this is not how money market funds work</em>. That is: In=C2=A0<em>most=
=C2=A0</em>situations =E2=80=94=C2=A0even situations like African Gold, whe=
re the executives are stealing from the SPAC =E2=80=94=C2=A0the shareholder=
s=E2=80=99 money in the SPAC really is safe, and they are guaranteed to get=
 their $10 back with interest. But=C2=A0<em>this=C2=A0</em>points to a situ=
ation where that isn=E2=80=99t true: If the SPAC owes money to legitimate v=
endors, those vendors are creditors who outrank the shareholders, and who c=
an claim money back from the shareholders. The trustee of the pot of money =
won=E2=80=99t give it to them =E2=80=94=C2=A0the trustee is laser-focused o=
n keeping the shareholders=E2=80=99 money safe =E2=80=94=C2=A0but a court p=
robably will. Courts know that SPAC shareholders are shareholders, and that=
 creditors outrank them.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here it=E2=80=99s 11 cents: The shareholders p=
ut in $10, got back $10.22, might have to pay back $0.11, and so end up wit=
h $10.11. They still get their money back with interest. But that=E2=80=99s=
 because the vendor bills are small. You could imagine another SPAC with a =
worse outcome: a big vendor bill that eats up all of the interest on the tr=
ust account and dips into principal, and a sponsor who is not good for the =
money. That is not very likely =E2=80=94=C2=A0SPACs tend not to run up huge=
 vendor bills, and if you are a vendor selling millions of dollars of stuff=
 to a SPAC you will probably want to do some due diligence to make sure it =
has the money to pay you =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is possible. Empirically, in=
 the SPAC boom of the 2020s, SPACs have been very good at giving shareholde=
rs back their $10. But that is not really a guarantee.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

5px;">Credit Suisse</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I do kind of think that part of the job of the=
=C2=A0chief executive officer of a bank is that, when there is a run on you=
r bank, you have to go on television and say confidently=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyes,=
 everything is fine, our assets are great and the bank run has stopped.=E2=
=80=9D If this is true =E2=80=94=C2=A0if your assets are great and the run =
has stopped =E2=80=94=C2=A0then, you know, great. If it is not true =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0if your assets are troubled and the run is ongoing =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
then, well, that=E2=80=99s why they pay you the big bucks.=C2=A0If you go o=
n television and=C2=A0<em>say=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=9Cyeah turns out our assets =
are mostly trash and people are pulling out their money as fast as they can=
,=E2=80=9D then that is definitely the end of your bank: After you say that=
, the run will intensify and you=E2=80=99ll be broke in an hour. If you go =
on television and say =E2=80=9Ceverything is great,=E2=80=9D and you are co=
nfident and poised enough, maybe the run will stop and you=E2=80=99ll have =
time to actually get back to normal.=C2=A0This stuff is not entirely self-f=
ulfilling =E2=80=94=C2=A0lots of bankers go on TV and say =E2=80=9Ceverythi=
ng is great=E2=80=9D and the run intensifies anyway and they go bankrupt =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is <em>partially=C2=A0</em>self-fulfilling, and your =
job as CEO really is to stop the run from intensifying.<a class=3D"footnote=
-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; d=
isplay: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is not legal advice =E2=80=94=C2=A0it is =
the opposite of legal advice! I bet your lawyer will tell you not to lie ab=
out your bank=E2=80=99s financial health on television! =E2=80=94=C2=A0and =
I am not even sure I believe it, but I kind of believe it.<a class=3D"footn=
ote-index" href=3D"#footnote-8" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: n=
one !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bor=
der-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important=
; display: inline-block;">
<span>[8]</span>
</a> You are the captain of the ship, which most days is a nice job and pay=
s well, but when the ship hits an iceberg you gotta be the last one on the =
bridge. By which I mean that potentially committing a certain amount of sec=
urities fraud on television is one of the hazards of the job.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly, if you are a bank=C2=A0<em>regulato=
r</em>, then I suspect that at some level you agree with this model =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0if the CEO of the biggest bank you regulate called you up and said=
 =E2=80=9Cthere=E2=80=99s a run on our bank, I=E2=80=99m gonna go on TV and=
 say =E2=80=98it=E2=80=99s all over, time to panic,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=
you would probably try to dissuade her<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#=
footnote-9" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: no=
ne !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-bl=
ock;">
<span>[9]</span>
</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0but you=E2=80=99d never=C2=A0<em>say=C2=A0</em>it. You =
can=E2=80=99t go around telling bank executives that it=E2=80=99s okay to l=
ie about their financial health in times of panic, first of all because tha=
t=E2=80=99s fraud and you can=E2=80=99t really encourage fraud, but also be=
cause if you=C2=A0<em>say=C2=A0</em>it publicly then it stops=C2=A0<em>work=
ing</em>. You can=E2=80=99t have a norm that is like =E2=80=9Cin a bank run=
, the bank can lie to you,=E2=80=9D because then in a bank run no one will =
believe the bank=E2=80=99s reassurances.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instead the right equilibrium is:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It is very illegal for senior executiv=
es at a bank to lie about their bank=E2=80=99s financial position, so any b=
ank executive who did that would be taking such an unthinkable personal ris=
k that no one believes they would actually do it; but</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">In the direst circumstances, maybe they do, a little bi=
t, and it works because it is so unthinkable that they=E2=80=99d be lying.<=
/li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/30629310.283439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGlj=
bGVzLzIwMjMtMDItMjEvY3JlZGl0LXN1aXNzZS1oaXRzLXJlY29yZC1sb3ctb24tcmVwb3J0LW9=

l1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwMjIyJnV0bV9jYW1wY=
Wlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB89d479c1" itemprop=3D"StoryLin=
k" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-=
decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color:=
 #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Anyway</a>:<=
/p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Credit Suisse Group AG slumped to a record low=
 on a report that the chairman is facing a probe over comments he made that=
 the firm had put a stop to huge client outflows after a run of share decli=
nes.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Swiss financial markets regulator Finma is see=
king to establish whether the comments from bank representatives including =
Chairman Axel Lehmann were misleading, according to a Reuters report. In a =
Bloomberg TV interview on Dec. 2, Lehmann said that outflows =E2=80=9Cbasic=
ally have stopped=E2=80=9D after it had disclosed on Nov. 23 the loss of 84=
 billion francs ($90.8 billion) of client assets. By the end of the quarter=
, that figure had risen to 110.5 billion francs.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The remarks were made before the close of a cr=
ucial $4 billion capital raise and helped arrest a sharp decline in the sha=
re price. Credit Suisse Chief Executive Officer Ulrich Koerner told analyst=
s on a call this month that more than 85% of the outflows came in October a=
nd November. That would leave as much as 17 billion francs coming in Decemb=
er after Lehmann=E2=80=99s remarks.=C2=A0=E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In cases of market manipulation, Switzerland=
=E2=80=99s financial regulator can invoke sanctions including banning indiv=
iduals from working in certain industries. However, it does not have the po=
wer to issue fines and cannot seize evidence or search premises.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Look,=C2=A0I assume that Lehmann=E2=80=99s com=
ments were true =E2=80=94=C2=A0or true enough; =E2=80=9Cbasically have stop=
ped=E2=80=9D leaves some room for imprecision =E2=80=94=C2=A0but even if th=
ey weren=E2=80=99t, they=C2=A0<em>worked=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0Credit Su=
isse raised capital and slowed outflows =E2=80=94=C2=A0and that is kind of =
the job. But then it=E2=80=99s also Finma=E2=80=99s job to investigate. Tho=
ugh possibly not to investigate all that hard:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99d think Finma =
would not be looking to further destabilize the bank by going after him agg=
ressively,=E2=80=9D Adam Terelak, an equity research analyst at Mediobanca,=
 said. =E2=80=9CThis just adds another headache for the bank that is strugg=
ling to stabilize itself post record client outflows.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p></b=
lockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elsewhere in <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=

style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;">modern financial regulatory expectations</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Europe=E2=80=99s top banking regulators have t=
old Deutsche Bank they are =E2=80=9Cnot satisfied=E2=80=9D with its probe i=
nto the mis-selling of risky foreign exchange derivatives in Spain, people =
briefed on the matter told the Financial Times.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Both the European Central Bank and German watc=
hdog BaFin have expressed frustration with the probe =E2=80=94 code-named =
=E2=80=9CProject Teal=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 which began in the second half of =
2019 and is about to be concluded, people familiar with the matter said.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The investigation found that Deutsche staff ac=
ted disingenuously, exploited flaws in the bank=E2=80=99s controls and brok=
e EU rules to sell highly complex foreign exchange derivatives to small and=
 medium-sized Spanish companies, the FT reported this month.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">However, regulators chided the group over seve=
ral issues, including shortcomings in methodology, as well as the length of=
 time it took to conduct the probe and then to punish those concerned. One =
person familiar with the matter said one of the points criticised by regula=
tors was that the bank only scrutinised a limited number of transactions wh=
en it checked the quality of its internal controls, arguing that taking a b=
roader sample would have been a better approach.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The traditional view of bank regulation is som=
ething like:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Banks do whatever they can=C2=A0to mak=
e a lot of money.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Regulators check=
 to make sure that they=E2=80=99re not making money in bad ways, and if the=
y are, they fine them.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the modern view of global bank regulation =
is more like:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Banks=E2=80=99 business units do whate=
ver they can to make a lot of money.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px=
;">Banks=E2=80=99 compliance, risk, etc., units check to make sure that the=
 business units aren=E2=80=99t making money in bad ways, and if they are, t=
hey turn them in to the regulators with a big show of contrition (and a big=
 fine).</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Regulators check to make s=
ure that the compliance units are appropriately watchful and/or contrite, a=
nd if they aren=E2=80=99t, they fine them.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The banks regulate themselves, and the regulat=
ors do <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30629310.28=
3439/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDItMD=
IvdGhlLXNlYy13YW50cy10by1zZWUtbW9yZS1waG9uZXM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDIyMjIzX01PTkVZU=
1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMw=
MjIyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB258443d3" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">  sort of meta-regulation</a> of the banks=E2=80=99 regulatory work. I=
t is more efficient to delegate the regulation to the banks: The banks have=
 bigger budgets, so they can hire more compliance staff, and the compliance=
 staff sit in the same building and probably have better access to the busi=
ness units than the regulators do. On the other hand they probably have=C2=
=A0<em>slightly=C2=A0</em>less incentive to be aggressive than the regulato=
rs do.=C2=A0</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the fall, about 45 call-center operators we=

ng to Yelisey Bohuslavskiy, chief research officer with the threat intellig=
ence firm Red Sense LLC.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They had been hired as part of a scam to talk =
potential victims into installing remote-access software onto networks that=
 would then be infected by ransomware, but the call centers ended up losing=
 money, he said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A lot of crime is not very businesslike, but r=
ansomware is an obviously businesslike crime =E2=80=94=C2=A0you need sales =
and customer service and compliance and a bunch of developers sitting at a =
computer =E2=80=94=C2=A0and it turns out to be a surprisingly normal busine=
ss. Ransomware companies are pretty much normal tech companies whose produc=
t is crime. And like many other tech companies these days, they=E2=80=99re =
doing layoffs.</p>
